title: Daily Briefing — Wednesday, May 20
tldr: Jack Clark's Oxford lecture: Anthropic's co-founder predicted a Nobel-worthy AI-assisted discovery within 12 months and a 60%+ chance of recursive self-improvement by end of 2028 — while naming a non-zero tail risk.
Google I/O after-shocks: Antigravity 2.0 had launch-day issues (IDE restored May 23); GitHub Copilot Chat purged Gemini and several GPT models from its web surface.

blocks.p: At Oxford, Jack Clark forecast a Nobel-level discovery with AI within 12 months and a 60%+ probability that AI helps train its successor (recursive self-improvement) by end of 2028 — alongside an explicit acknowledgment of tail risk.
blocks.why: The people closest to the frontier are planning for very fast capability gains; calibrate your own timeline accordingly.
blocks.doIt: Build skills that compound with stronger models (orchestration, evals, judgment) rather than skills they replace.

blocks.p: Google's Antigravity 2.0 shipped with day-one problems (the IDE was restored May 23); GitHub Copilot Chat removed all Gemini models plus GPT-5.2 Codex and GPT-5.4 nano from its web surface (VS Code/JetBrains unaffected).
blocks.why: Platform model availability changes weekly — don't hard-couple a workflow to one vendor's lineup.
blocks.doIt: Keep an abstraction layer between your tools and any single model.

sources: buildfastwithai (May 20 roundup) · Anthropic · GitHub Changelog · Google (forward-looking claims are directional).
